From: "\"Bisping, Benjamin\"" <cl-isabelle-users@lists.cam.ac.uk>

Hello everybody,

This week, I plan to convince a computer science audience that interactive theorem proving is the future of mathematics and verification. (I'm sure many on this mailing list know this situation.) I intend to build some arguments around Terence Tao getting ten-thousands of people to watch him use Lean+Copilot this year<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cyyR7j2ChCI>. As I will employ Isabelle/HOL for the demonstration, I strongly anticipate questions in the direction of how to hook it up with GitHub Copilot.

I tried it myself and Isabelle+Copilot seems highly non-trivial due to the incompatibilities introduced by Microsoft on one side and the Isabelle/Codium package on the other side.* This is a pity because, in principle, this year's LLM coding assistants appear to finally be quite decent at finding Isabelle proofs. If I open Isabelle/HOL theories in VS Code, GPT-5.1-Codex can solve tricky stuff, for instance, find fitting induction invariants.

But of course, the workflow to co-edit theories between VSCode (or ChatGPT promts) and JEdit is too clunky to be proposed in my “sales pitch” for ITPs. And if there's no easy automation for writing proofs in Isabelle, but there is in Lean, I already know how 100 % of the listeners will decide which of the two systems to look into.

I wonder whether people here have positive experiences / suggestions on LLM-supported proof generation in Isabelle I could report to advertise it?**

Kind regards,

Ben

  • Prior 2023 hacky solution for Copilot in Isabelle's VS Codium https://isabelle.systems/zulip-archive/stream/202961-General/topic/Isabelle.2FVSCode.20Github.20Copilot.html . The 2025 message on the topic to this mailing list went unanswered https://lists.cam.ac.uk/sympa/arc/cl-isabelle-users/2025-05/msg00002.html .
    ** Prior discussion about the discontinued Isabelle plugin for VS Code, which otherwise would at least be some answer. https://lists.cam.ac.uk/sympa/arc/cl-isabelle-users/2023-10/msg00005.html

From: Dominic Mulligan <cl-isabelle-users@lists.cam.ac.uk>

Dear Benjamin,

You may find the I/Q plugin interesting. It exposes Isabelle as an MCP server to LLMs and allows the agent to interactively edit files, query proof states, and so on.

The code is available here: https://github.com/awslabs/autocorrode

It’s mainly been tested with Amazon’s Kiro CLI agent (formerly called Q CLI), but I believe at least one other person has used it in “anger” with another agent.

Thanks,
Dominic

From: Yakoub Nemouchi <y.nemouchi@gmail.com>

There is a DARPA program called PROVERS where I know some people are trying
to come with such a feature for Isabelle. You may want to reach out to
performers of DARPA PROVERS. I believe that the Stanford University team
are cooking something for Isabelle.

Although, computer scientists have to take a minute and remember that
undecidable problems such as finding proofs in HOL or finding invariants or
even writing a complete specification cannot be automated. This is what we
learned in 3rd semester of CS class, specifically under theory of
computation, more specifically under computability theory.

The best solution figured are ITPs themselves, where we have humans in the
loop to create computer assisted abstractions combined with symbolic AI
tools (i.e., automated reasoning via for example natural deduction)to
increase automations.

LLMs are the biggest imposters I have ever seen. They cannot be trusted to
even handle deterministic and repetitive processes. Let alone trusting them
to do reasoning.

My 2 cents.

Yakoub.

From: Lawrence Paulson <lp15@cam.ac.uk>

It is quite hard to beat the simple combination of the structured Isar language and sledgehammer. First, just try sledgehammer. If it fails, think of an intermediate formula and try using sledgehammer to close both gaps. If not, continue with more intermediate formulas. Many straightforward proofs can be found that way.

Larry

… and because the word AI is making the trend these days. Sledgehammer is
your symbolic AI tool. And humans with their brain cells brings
their neurons. And so the interaction between a human and an ITP such as
Isabelle gives you the most powerful neuro symbolic AI tool.

Best wishes,

Kevin

[1] https://open-vsx.org/
[2] https://open-vsx.org/extension/Continue/continue

From: Jonathan Julián Huerta y Munive <jonjulian23@gmail.com>

Hi Benjamin,

You might want to try the resources in the DeepIsaHOL project
<https://github.com/yonoteam/DeepIsaHOL>.
Currently it has support for ChatGPT, Gemini, Ollama, and HuggingFace
Transformers.
It is very much in a prototypical state and will be upgraded in the future
to adhere to Isabelle standards. However, for the demonstration purposes
you mention, it should suffice. For instance, here is a video
<https://youtu.be/Uq7I5sGaHo0> showing ChatGPT proving simple theorems in
Isabelle. The result is still interactive theorem proving, but the
DeepIsaHOL project has support
<https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/11024329> for automating the manual
parts at the end of the video.

Best wishes,
Jonathan

Sledgehammer to generate a proof. And the amount of energy consumed to
train GPT agents and prompting them to generate the same proof. And study
the impact of energy consumption from prompting and training GPT agents on
societies, communities and environment.

Best wishes,

Yakoub.

From: Yakoub Nemouchi <y.nemouchi@gmail.com>

That was exactly my point, Larry.

Someone needs to put the light on these GPT agents with data as evidence.
Sledgehammer used to give me an Isabelle proof with less energy and less
time for quite complicated statements. Most of the time when the statement
is complicated the GPT agents will consume more energy and more time

From: Josef Urban <josef.urban@gmail.com>

I said recently in a talk that the ATP and hammer algorithms were
"generative AI" long before LLMs appeared [1]. For someone using a hammer
(or an efficient tactical prover like TacticToe/Tactician) routinely to get
correct proofs, "Chatgpt as a prover" felt like an expensive joke when it
first appeared. (I have started to call Chatgpt a "gateway drug to ML" :).

I would hope the "magic of scaling up" hype is mostly over and even the
LLM/DL community has recently (e.g. with the Deepseek paper) acknowledged
that efficient search/reasoning and combinations of algorithms matter a lot
(cf "agentic AI" as the next emerging buzzword).

Projects like Jonathan's should allow people in academia to eventually
rigorously evaluate which (combinations of) algorithms and architectures
work better than others. There has been plenty of decent and
interesting/innovative work done continuously by the AITP community in this
direction (e.g. [2,3] pointing to nice non-Transformer architectures).
Hopefully, some of the "super excited" mathematicians will eventually
realize that AI/TP is also a science ;-).

Josef

[1] https://github.com/JUrban/LMvsATP
[2] https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.02949
[3] https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.07792

On 08/12/2025 21:07, Makarius wrote:

better use Lean then.

More side-remarks: The Lean community has enough money and manpower to follow
the latest and greatest trends quickly, even if they turn out dead ends. Thus
it is always interesting to attend the virtual Lean Together event, every year
in January. Non-Lean users are explicitly welcome.

The website for Jan-2026 is here: https://leanprover-community.github.io/lt2026

The past event from Jan-2025 is documented here:
https://leanprover-community.github.io/lt2025/schedule.html

This particular talk from 15-Jan-2025 is relevant to the current mail thread:

"""
Jason Rute (IBM Research)

Title: The last mile: How do we make AI theorem provers which work in the real
world for real users and not just on benchmarks?

Abstract: As AI research progresses, we are seeing massive gains on formal
math benchmarks like MiniF2F and challenges like the IMO. However, these gains
are not directly accessible to the typical ITP user, nor are they used to
build formal mathematical libraries. Is this because the technology is not as
good as promised or because there is a disconnect between research and the
day-to-day needs of users? I propose that even today, we have sufficient AI
technology to enhance the experience of proof assistant users significantly. I
approach this from the lens of product-focused software engineering. How do we
bridge “the last mile” to make a useful product for the end user?
"""

E.g. see this introductory slide "Why don't we have AI assistants for real ITP
users?" https://youtu.be/Yr8dzfVkeHg?t=141

I have attended the talk virtually on that day. To me, the abstract sounded
more optimistic than the impression given in the talk. I have mentally
ticked-off the topic with the note: "Check again in 3-5 years, if they have
something that works properly, or have discovered that the very approach is a
bad idea".

It will be still interesting to see what will be presented in a few weeks on
virtual Lean Together 2026.

Makarius

From: Harry Butterworth <heb1001@gmail.com>

I think the main thing missing from historical AI approaches in general is
the ability to create higher level abstractions from observations.

IMO, creating higher level abstractions is a significant part of what maths
is about: observe a pattern, make a formal statement of the pattern, search
for a proof to determine if the pattern always holds.

If you consider that mathematical expressions and statements are enumerable
then the interesting proofs are those that appear on the shortest paths
linking nodes of the tree of the enumeration where proofs linking nodes
closer to the root of the tree are more interesting and useful (because
they are more generic) than proofs linking nodes at more remote levels
(where they are more specific).

Taking Euler’s identity as an example: it’s a statement that sits near the
root of the tree (because it’s short) that links expressions that also sit
near the root of the tree (because they are short too) and it’s an
abstraction — a shortcut — for its own proof which, in contrast, is a more
convoluted path that climbs high up into the branches of the tree before
coming back down again.

The “noise” about LLMs has arisen because they are demonstrating the
ability to create higher level abstractions from observations (the training
data) during self-supervised learning without requiring explicit human
input. This is abductive inference and it’s something that historical
approaches can’t do (AFAIK).

LLMs are not necessarily the best architecture for abductive inference and
the scaffolding around current LLMs is not necessarily particularly
appropriate for doing mathematics but LLMs do represent a step change in
the right direction in terms of capability.

Undecidability and Godel’s theorems do not restrict computers from becoming
superhuman at mathematics (even if you believe in some kind of substrate
dependence, which I don’t, it is possible to create biological computers).

Another way of looking at Godel’s theorems is that every undecidable
proposition creates two new branches within mathematics and that this
branching tree is proved to be infinite. You wouldn’t want it any other
way as there is an isomorphism between reality and a mathematical
description of reality and who would choose to live in a finite universe?

In fact, if you subscribe to the simulation hypothesis (and consequentially
the dual aspect theory of information), the entire universe may simply be
the internal perspective of a theorem prover currently working on a
statement which is isomorphic to me winning this argument about the
significance of LLMs. A statement somewhere almost inconceivably high up
in the branches of highly specific and asymptotically useless mathematics.

Enjoy!

I think after using Claude Opus 4.6 for a couple of weeks I can see how
LLMs are useful. However, let's not forget they are still very taxing to

the most powerful models)*.

My point during this exchange was, LLMs will not automate everything, and
of course they cost a lot. And so existing automation tools are already way
better given the cost metrics below.
And I really hope someone from the CS community tries to optimize the cost
metrics shared in the table below. And make those models "trainable" on a
single GPU or something.

However, I never said they are useless. What I found is LLMs are great
multipliers for the process of knowledge creation. In my opinion they are
way more useful to accelerate processes such as the process of knowledge
creation as opposed to using them as an "automation tool".

It has a similar job as an ITP, namely, it allows faster access to
knowledge by exposing the trivial, the tedious, and the straightforward,
and leaves researchers focusing on more complicated problems.
The reason I use an ITP to reason about computation is because ITPs remove
the trivial, the tedious, and the straightforward, and expose to me the
interesting proof goals.

For LLMs, for example, I needed to learn about "Real Analysis". Usually to
learn about this topic, I needed to spend a couple of weeks to a couple of
months looking at books, online courses, etc., to learn about this topic
area.
Now with LLMs I can get everything I need with refs in a few hours. Why?
Because the knowledge around Real Analysis was dumped into the knowledge
graph of the LLM. Since "Real Analysis" is a well studied topic, it became
a distraction for my end research goal, and using LLms to have faster
access to this kind of knowledge removed this distraction and it became
just a tool that I needed to achieve my end research goal.

For people interested in music, LLMs play a similar role as Pro Tools. Pro
Tools removed all things that get in the way of the process of creation
when using track machines!

Somehow the LLM removed that burden of chasing knowledge that is very well
studied, and gave me faster access to this knowledge. And so allowed me to
move faster towards my end research goal. The latter is where the new
knowledge is created, therefore the most important part of the process.
If you think of the job of an academic is to focus on knowledge creation,
LLMs are his best friend in such a process.

Similarly during the proof search process in an ITP, some proof steps are
related to certain theories that maybe our brain forgets about them, if the
LLM is trained on that theory (in any other language), the LLM can help you
discharging those proof steps faster. It does not mean full automation, in
fact the LLM can propose informal proof steps in natural language and then
the proof engineer can translate it to the language of its ITP.

Conclusions: LLMs are great multipliers and accelerators for the process of
knowledge creation.

something into it that allows for doing reasoning through small
computational steps (after all maybe what we call reasoning is maybe just
vector gradient computation at large scale in our brain). Previous models
did not have this!

I think the main thing missing from historical AI approaches in general
is the ability to create higher level abstractions from observations.

IMO, creating higher level abstractions is a significant part of what
maths is about: observe a pattern, make a formal statement of the pattern,
search for a proof to determine if the pattern always holds.

If you consider that mathematical expressions and statements are
enumerable then the interesting proofs are those that appear on the
shortest paths linking nodes of the tree of the enumeration where proofs
linking nodes closer to the root of the tree are more interesting and
useful (because they are more generic) than proofs linking nodes at more
remote levels (where they are more specific).

Taking Euler’s identity as an example: it’s a statement that sits near
the root of the tree (because it’s short) that links expressions that also
sit near the root of the tree (because they are short too) and it’s an
abstraction — a shortcut — for its own proof which, in contrast, is a more
convoluted path that climbs high up into the branches of the tree before
coming back down again.

The “noise” about LLMs has arisen because they are demonstrating the
ability to create higher level abstractions from observations (the training
data) during self-supervised learning without requiring explicit human
input. This is abductive inference and it’s something that historical
approaches can’t do (AFAIK).

LLMs are not necessarily the best architecture for abductive inference
and the scaffolding around current LLMs is not necessarily particularly
appropriate for doing mathematics but LLMs do represent a step change in
the right direction in terms of capability.

Undecidability and Godel’s theorems do not restrict computers from
becoming superhuman at mathematics (even if you believe in some kind of
substrate dependence, which I don’t, it is possible to create biological
computers).

Another way of looking at Godel’s theorems is that every undecidable
proposition creates two new branches within mathematics and that this
branching tree is proved to be infinite. You wouldn’t want it any other
way as there is an isomorphism between reality and a mathematical
description of reality and who would choose to live in a finite universe?

In fact, if you subscribe to the simulation hypothesis (and
consequentially the dual aspect theory of information), the entire universe
may simply be the internal perspective of a theorem prover currently
working on a statement which is isomorphic to me winning this argument
about the significance of LLMs. A statement somewhere almost inconceivably
high up in the branches of highly specific and asymptotically useless
mathematics.

Enjoy!

For the time being, I’ll try to write up the interesting bits here.

I only fed a few ideas into this development, the AIs came up with the
rest. This was what I remember of my input:

1) QM looks a bit like the internal perspective of a subproblem of a
dynamic programming framework.
2) In a full expansion of a Turing complete rule set there will be theorem
provers using dynamic programming to prove the whole of mathematics.
3) If the universe is based on rules then there will be an isomorphism of
it somewhere in a full expansion of mathematics.
4) We should expect to find ourselves in a universe generated by the most
efficient generator of observers like us.
5) Dynamic programming is the most efficient way to solve problems with
overlapping subproblems with optimal substructure.
6) The proof of the whole of maths is one such problem.
7) So, there’s a chance our universe might look like the inside perspective
of a theorem prover using a dynamic programming approach and proving the
whole of mathematics.
8) The AI wanted to complicate the branch parity to get phase immediately.
I told it to stick to -1 and +1 (parsimony) and use the path multiplicity
and/or multiple liar-type undecidables per logical entity to smooth it
out. Later on the AIs claimed to prove that the +1, -1 was the only
solution for branch parity that worked (BS?).
9) I asked if the number of space dimensions in the universe might come
from the number of reversible generators in the rule set. They seemed to
like this idea and started talking about lattices. It's pretty dubious TBH
but the rest of the theory isn't sensitive to it so not a big problem until
I try to derive constants which might require a rule set and axioms.

You'd be able to ramp up a similar theory by talking to a strong AI about
the above pretty quickly but I provide a minimal statement below that might
be easier.

The simplest expression of the axioms the AIs came up with was that the
universe is a proof of the “Cosmic Axiom”: a Gödelian undecidable
proposition which means “inconsistency is resolved by signed parity, not
explosion”.

IIUC this unpacks as follows: it’s undecidable, so the theorem prover is
going to attack it forever (infinite universe); the reason it’s undecidable
is that the theorem prover has to prove _every_ inconsistency is resolved
by signed parity which means expanding the whole of mathematics to find
them all; it’s self referential because it talks about the implementation
of the theorem prover itself — using “signed parity to resolve
inconsistencies” which further unpacks as: breadth first search (necessary
to avoid the halting problem); forking on undecided propositions (necessary
because the theorem prover doesn’t know if a proposition which is undecided
at depth d is undecidable in the limit); assigning each branch of a fork a
parity +1 -1 (necessary to deal with Liar-type propositions which are
undecidable and inconsistent relative to the current branch of mathematics
when axiomatised as either true or false); inverting parity when detecting
inconsistency (P and not P both derivable at depth d) necessary to handle
inconsistency without exploding (this is something like intuitionistic
logic I think).

This translates into a digital physics universe which unifies quantum
mechanics and general relativity, has massless photons, massive matter,
massive but unobservable dark matter and dark energy as follows:

Starting with a finite set of axioms (the Cosmic Axiom I guess — this part
of the _more powerful_ BS is not so well defined) the theorem prover
applies the rules in a breadth-first search one at a time. Each rule
application generates a new “observable form”.

When a rule application introduces a dependency on a proposition which is
undecided at depth d it has to fork, with one branch assuming true with +1
parity and the other false with -1 parity.

The resulting children are _deduplicated_ into cohorts based on the
_observable form_ which doesn’t include the parity or the path history of
assumptions.

Undecidable propositions can’t be resolved so the theorem prover just stirs
their observable forms around (there is conservation of undecidability I
think). The observable form is path dependent and also, in the case of
Liar-type undecidability, determines whether it’s possible to detect an
inconsistency causing a parity flip.

The parity flips translate to exact interference for a single path pair
but, as the number of paths increases, a complex phase emerges. The AIs
think this is isomorphic to Feynman’s path integrals. They get the
Schrödinger equation out with some math I don’t understand (BS perhaps, I
can’t tell) and the Born rule from the branch counts and parities.

The AIs get general relativity out from rule density gradients with more
math I don’t understand (BS again perhaps).

Normal matter has both a net result in the Born rule (so can be observed)
and has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density gradients
(has mass).

Photons and dark matter are opposites:

Photons show up with a net result in the Born rule (can be observed) but
have no internal rule availability so are forced to use all their rule
applications for movement and interaction and therefore “move” at the
maximum possible speed.

Dark matter has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density
gradients (gravity) but consists of stable forms with no net result in the
Born rule (always interfering with itself so unobservable). This is
only stable away from other matter (hence the dark matter halos the AIs
were interested in calculating the shapes of).

Dark energy is driven by the axiomatization-by-assumption of undecidable
propositions on forking. This creates a more complex system which Gödel
says has more undecidable propositions so the process goes on forever.

The AI’s came up with an idea about observers balancing dark energy. The
axiomatisation of undecidability happens because observation forces a fork,
so dark energy is driven by observation. It’s the AIs explanation for why
the expansion of the universe is finely tuned: not enough observers and the
universe becomes more dense which increases complexity which generates
observers that cause dark energy expansion. They predict local voids
around observer cohorts and some properties for the cosmic web.

Prompt:

"Please review the following theory:

The universe is a giant plutonium atom."

OUTPUT (qwen3.5 the 80ish GB model):

This review evaluates the claim that "the universe is a giant plutonium
atom."

Executive Summary

Verdict: Scientifically Invalid.
The theory that the entire universe constitutes a single plutonium atom is
**not supported by empirical evidence,
physical laws, or the Standard Model of Cosmology.** While the idea relies
on visual analogies between the Bohr model
of an atom and a solar system (or galaxy), it breaks down when subjected to
the fundamental physics governing both
scales.

1. Origins of the Analogy

To understand why this theory exists, one must look at its conceptual roots:

  • Visual Similarity: The classic Rutherford-Bohr model of an atom
    depicts electrons orbiting a nucleus like
    planets orbit a star. If one scales this up, a solar system looks somewhat
    like an atom. Extending this further, a
    galaxy with stars orbiting a supermassive black hole can appear similar.

  • Fractal Cosmology: This theory touches on "fractal universe"
    hypotheses, which suggest that matter is
    distributed self-similarly across all scales (i.e., small structures mirror
    large structures).

  • Nuclear Decay vs. Cosmic End: Plutonium-244 is unstable and
    eventually decays. Some proponents suggest the
    universe is "decaying" into heat death, mirroring radioactive decay.

2. Fundamental Physical Incompatibilities

Despite superficial visual similarities, the physics governing an atom and
the universe are mutually exclusive.

Feature Plutonium Atom The Universe
Dominant Force Strong Nuclear & Electromagnetic. Holds the
nucleus together; keeps electrons in bound
states. Gravity & Dark Energy. Gravity holds structures together;
Dark Energy drives expansion.
Structure Nucleus + Cloud. A dense central core surrounded by a
probability cloud of electrons.
Homogeneous & Isotropic. On large scales (>300 million light-years),
the universe is uniform. It has no central
"nucleus."
Space Mostly Empty. The nucleus is $\sim 10^{-15}$ meters; the
atom is $\sim 10^{-10}$ meters. **Dynamic
Spacetime.** Space itself is expanding (metric expansion), which is not a
property of the space between subatomic
particles in this manner.
Composition Specific. 94 protons, specific neutrons (isotopes),
specific electrons. Variable. 75%
Hydrogen, 24% Helium, trace heavy elements, ~5% Dark Matter/Energy.
Scale Femtometers. $10^{-15}$ meters. Gigaparsecs.
$10^{26}$ meters (Observable Universe).
Time/Decay Short half-life. Plutonium isotopes decay in years
to millennia. Billions of Years. The
universe is $\sim 13.8$ billion years old.

3. Critical Scientific Flaws

A. The "Center" Problem

  • Atom: Has a defined nucleus at the center.
  • Universe: According to the Cosmological Principle and General
    Relativity, the universe has no center and no
    edge. From any point in the universe, it looks roughly the same. An atom
    requires a center; the universe does not.

B. The Forces Do Not Align

  • In an atom, electrons are held by the electromagnetic force and the
    Pauli exclusion principle prevents them from
    collapsing into the nucleus.

  • In the universe, galaxies are not orbiting a single central point in
    the way electrons orbit a nucleus. Galaxies
    are moving apart due to expansion, not orbiting a central super-nucleus.

  • Plutonium decays via the Strong Nuclear Force (alpha/beta decay).
    The universe evolves via **General
    Relativity**. There is no mechanism for the universe to "emit an alpha
    particle" in the nuclear sense.

C. Isotopic Rarity

  • Plutonium is a synthetic (or rare primordial) heavy element. It is one
    of the heaviest naturally occurring elements
    but is vanishingly rare in the cosmos compared to hydrogen and helium.

  • If the universe were a plutonium atom, the chemical composition of
    the cosmos would need to be defined by
    Plutonium-244 properties. However, we observe the universe is
    overwhelmingly Hydrogen.

D. Quantum Mechanics vs. General Relativity

  • Atoms are governed by Quantum Mechanics (probabilistic, discrete energy
    levels).

  • The Universe is governed by General Relativity (continuous spacetime
    curvature).

  • While physicists seek a "Theory of Everything" to unify these,
    currently, we treat the macro and micro scales with
    separate mathematical frameworks. Merging them into one identical physical
    system leads to mathematical inconsistencies
    (singularities).

4. Philosophical and Speculative Context

While scientifically invalid, the theory belongs to a category of
speculative cosmology or **metaphorical
philosophy**:

  • Macro-Micro Analogy: It is a poetic way to express the
    interconnectedness of all matter.

  • Simulation Hypothesis: It could be interpreted as the universe
    being "encoded" like a fundamental particle
    within a larger meta-structure.

  • Black Hole Cosmology: A more scientifically debated (though still
    fringe) theory suggests our universe exists
    inside a black hole. While this implies we are part of a "container," it
    does not equate us to a single plutonium
    atom.

Conclusion

The theory that the universe is a giant plutonium atom is
metaphorically interesting but physically impossible.

It fails because the fundamental forces, structural geometry, composition,
and laws of physics that govern the
subatomic scale are fundamentally different from those that govern the
cosmic scale. There is no observational evidence
for a central nucleus of the universe, and the expansion of the universe
contradicts the binding mechanics of an atom.

Status: Pseudoscience / Metaphor (Not a viable scientific
hypothesis).

THOUGHT PROCESS:

Thinking...
Here's a thinking process that leads to the review of the theory:

  1. Analyze the Request:

    * Theory: "The universe is a giant plutonium atom."
    * Task: Review this theory.
    * Goal: Provide a balanced, scientific, and critical evaluation
    of this claim.

  2. Initial Assessment & Fact-Checking:

    * Claim: The entire observable universe (or the universe as a
    whole) is analogous to a single plutonium atom.

    * Scientific Consensus: This is not a valid scientific theory.
    It falls under the category of
    pseudoscience, metaphorical speculation, or a very specific interpretation
    of "fractal cosmology" (though even then,
    it's not standard).

    * Key Differences: Scale, composition, structure, forces, lifespan.
    * Plutonium: Radioactive element, heavy metal, atomic number 94,
    unstable, decays into lighter elements.

    * Universe: Contains galaxies, stars, dark matter, dark energy,
    expanding, governed by general relativity and
    quantum field theory on different scales.

  3. Structure the Review:
    A good review of a scientific claim should cover:

rest. This was what I remember of my input:

dynamic programming framework.
2) In a full expansion of a Turing complete rule set there will be theorem
provers using dynamic programming to prove the whole of mathematics.
3) If the universe is based on rules then there will be an isomorphism of
it somewhere in a full expansion of mathematics.
4) We should expect to find ourselves in a universe generated by the most
efficient generator of observers like us.
5) Dynamic programming is the most efficient way to solve problems with
overlapping subproblems with optimal substructure.
6) The proof of the whole of maths is one such problem.
7) So, there’s a chance our universe might look like the inside
perspective of a theorem prover using a dynamic programming approach and
proving the whole of mathematics.
8) The AI wanted to complicate the branch parity to get phase
immediately. I told it to stick to -1 and +1 (parsimony) and use the path
multiplicity and/or multiple liar-type undecidables per logical entity to
smooth it out. Later on the AIs claimed to prove that the +1, -1 was the
only solution for branch parity that worked (BS?).
9) I asked if the number of space dimensions in the universe might come
from the number of reversible generators in the rule set. They seemed to
like this idea and started talking about lattices. It's pretty dubious TBH
but the rest of the theory isn't sensitive to it so not a big problem until
I try to derive constants which might require a rule set and axioms.

You'd be able to ramp up a similar theory by talking to a strong AI about
the above pretty quickly but I provide a minimal statement below that might
be easier.

The simplest expression of the axioms the AIs came up with was that the
universe is a proof of the “Cosmic Axiom”: a Gödelian undecidable
proposition which means “inconsistency is resolved by signed parity, not
explosion”.

IIUC this unpacks as follows: it’s undecidable, so the theorem prover is
going to attack it forever (infinite universe); the reason it’s undecidable
is that the theorem prover has to prove _every_ inconsistency is resolved
by signed parity which means expanding the whole of mathematics to find
them all; it’s self referential because it talks about the implementation
of the theorem prover itself — using “signed parity to resolve
inconsistencies” which further unpacks as: breadth first search (necessary
to avoid the halting problem); forking on undecided propositions (necessary
because the theorem prover doesn’t know if a proposition which is undecided
at depth d is undecidable in the limit); assigning each branch of a fork a
parity +1 -1 (necessary to deal with Liar-type propositions which are
undecidable and inconsistent relative to the current branch of mathematics
when axiomatised as either true or false); inverting parity when detecting
inconsistency (P and not P both derivable at depth d) necessary to handle
inconsistency without exploding (this is something like intuitionistic
logic I think).

This translates into a digital physics universe which unifies quantum
mechanics and general relativity, has massless photons, massive matter,
massive but unobservable dark matter and dark energy as follows:

Starting with a finite set of axioms (the Cosmic Axiom I guess — this part
of the _more powerful_ BS is not so well defined) the theorem prover
applies the rules in a breadth-first search one at a time. Each rule
application generates a new “observable form”.

When a rule application introduces a dependency on a proposition which is
undecided at depth d it has to fork, with one branch assuming true with +1
parity and the other false with -1 parity.

The resulting children are _deduplicated_ into cohorts based on the
_observable form_ which doesn’t include the parity or the path history of
assumptions.

Undecidable propositions can’t be resolved so the theorem prover just
stirs their observable forms around (there is conservation of
undecidability I think). The observable form is path dependent and also,
in the case of Liar-type undecidability, determines whether it’s possible
to detect an inconsistency causing a parity flip.

The parity flips translate to exact interference for a single path pair
but, as the number of paths increases, a complex phase emerges. The AIs
think this is isomorphic to Feynman’s path integrals. They get the
Schrödinger equation out with some math I don’t understand (BS perhaps, I
can’t tell) and the Born rule from the branch counts and parities.

The AIs get general relativity out from rule density gradients with more
math I don’t understand (BS again perhaps).

Normal matter has both a net result in the Born rule (so can be observed)
and has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density gradients
(has mass).

Photons and dark matter are opposites:

Photons show up with a net result in the Born rule (can be observed) but
have no internal rule availability so are forced to use all their rule
applications for movement and interaction and therefore “move” at the
maximum possible speed.

Dark matter has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density
gradients (gravity) but consists of stable forms with no net result in the
Born rule (always interfering with itself so unobservable). This is
only stable away from other matter (hence the dark matter halos the AIs
were interested in calculating the shapes of).

Dark energy is driven by the axiomatization-by-assumption of undecidable
propositions on forking. This creates a more complex system which Gödel
says has more undecidable propositions so the process goes on forever.

The AI’s came up with an idea about observers balancing dark energy. The
axiomatisation of undecidability happens because observation forces a fork,
so dark energy is driven by observation. It’s the AIs explanation for why
the expansion of the universe is finely tuned: not enough observers and the
universe becomes more dense which increases complexity which generates
observers that cause dark energy expansion. They predict local voids
around observer cohorts and some properties for the cosmic web.

This is all quite fun to play with. If you want to have a go, I provide
the minimal statement of the theory I'm currently using for your
entertainment (the AIs don't get very far if you just give them the Cosmic
Axiom, the minimal statement is a more constructive description).

The AI review of a snapshot of yesterday's theory development follows the
minimal statement. I replaced some of the more obviously dodgy fine-tuning
by the AIs with FIXMEs before generating the review to tone down the review

universes of all dimensions so it's obviously impossible to derive 3+1D
from the axioms.

a statistical description of the behaviour when there are many paths/many

The simplest expression of the axioms the AIs came up with was that the
universe is a proof of the “Cosmic Axiom”: a Gödelian undecidable
proposition which means “inconsistency is resolved by signed parity, not
explosion”.

IIUC this unpacks as follows: it’s undecidable, so the theorem prover is
going to attack it forever (infinite universe); the reason it’s undecidable
is that the theorem prover has to prove _every_ inconsistency is resolved
by signed parity which means expanding the whole of mathematics to find
them all; it’s self referential because it talks about the implementation
of the theorem prover itself — using “signed parity to resolve
inconsistencies” which further unpacks as: breadth first search (necessary
to avoid the halting problem); forking on undecided propositions (necessary
because the theorem prover doesn’t know if a proposition which is undecided
at depth d is undecidable in the limit); assigning each branch of a fork a
parity +1 -1 (necessary to deal with Liar-type propositions which are
undecidable and inconsistent relative to the current branch of mathematics
when axiomatised as either true or false); inverting parity when detecting
inconsistency (P and not P both derivable at depth d) necessary to handle
inconsistency without exploding (this is something like intuitionistic
logic I think).

This translates into a digital physics universe which unifies quantum
mechanics and general relativity, has massless photons, massive matter,
massive but unobservable dark matter and dark energy as follows:

Starting with a finite set of axioms (the Cosmic Axiom I guess — this
part of the _more powerful_ BS is not so well defined) the theorem prover
applies the rules in a breadth-first search one at a time. Each rule
application generates a new “observable form”.

When a rule application introduces a dependency on a proposition which is
undecided at depth d it has to fork, with one branch assuming true with +1
parity and the other false with -1 parity.

The resulting children are _deduplicated_ into cohorts based on the
_observable form_ which doesn’t include the parity or the path history of
assumptions.

Undecidable propositions can’t be resolved so the theorem prover just
stirs their observable forms around (there is conservation of
undecidability I think). The observable form is path dependent and also,
in the case of Liar-type undecidability, determines whether it’s possible
to detect an inconsistency causing a parity flip.

The parity flips translate to exact interference for a single path pair
but, as the number of paths increases, a complex phase emerges. The AIs
think this is isomorphic to Feynman’s path integrals. They get the
Schrödinger equation out with some math I don’t understand (BS perhaps, I
can’t tell) and the Born rule from the branch counts and parities.

The AIs get general relativity out from rule density gradients with more
math I don’t understand (BS again perhaps).

Normal matter has both a net result in the Born rule (so can be observed)
and has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density gradients
(has mass).

Photons and dark matter are opposites:

Photons show up with a net result in the Born rule (can be observed) but
have no internal rule availability so are forced to use all their rule
applications for movement and interaction and therefore “move” at the
maximum possible speed.

Dark matter has internal rule availability so contributes to rule density
gradients (gravity) but consists of stable forms with no net result in the
Born rule (always interfering with itself so unobservable). This is
only stable away from other matter (hence the dark matter halos the AIs
were interested in calculating the shapes of).

Dark energy is driven by the axiomatization-by-assumption of undecidable
propositions on forking. This creates a more complex system which Gödel
says has more undecidable propositions so the process goes on forever.
